covered_sections: 2.1 Projectile motion>Equations for the horizontal component of motion
2.1 Projectile motion>Horizontal range and maximum height of a projectile

mini_headings_covered: Equations for the horizontal component of motion
Horizontal range and maximum height of a projectile
Relation between range and maximum height

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What term describes the curved path followed by a projectile under the influence of gravity alone, ignoring air resistance?
questions.hint: This word describes the specific curved shape of the projectile's path through the air.
questions.explanation: Trajectory is the path a projectile follows under gravity, typically parabolic when launched horizontally or at an angle. Option B (displacement) is the straight-line change in position, not the curved path.

source_trace.content_anchor: the path of a projectile, which we call its trajectory, is a parabola

explanations_tiered.definition: A trajectory is the curved path that a projectile follows when moving under the influence of gravity alone.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Projectiles follow parabolic trajectories because horizontal velocity remains constant while vertical motion is uniformly accelerated by gravity.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a basketball thrown toward a hoop. The curved arc it makes through the air is its trajectory, like a rainbow shape.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A trajectory is the parabolic path resulting from independent horizontal (constant velocity) and vertical (constant acceleration) motions. The shape is a parabola.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The trajectory equation for horizontal projection is \( y = \frac{g}{2v_{0x}^2}x^2 \), showing parabolic dependence of vertical displacement \(y\) on horizontal displacement \(x\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that projectile motion combines constant horizontal velocity with vertical acceleration g.
The combination of these motions produces a curved path.
That curved path has a specific name: trajectory.
misconceptions: Some believe trajectory is always a straight line, but gravity curves the path downward.
Others confuse trajectory with displacement; displacement is the straight line from start to end, not the path.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Trajectory: curved parabolic path of a projectile under gravity.

glossary_entry.definition: The curved path followed by a projectile under the influence of gravity alone, ignoring air resistance.

flashcard.front: What is the curved path of a projectile called?
flashcard.back: Trajectory — the parabolic path resulting from constant horizontal velocity and vertical acceleration \(g\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The curved parabolic path of a projectile under gravity, combining constant horizontal motion with uniformly accelerated vertical motion.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The curved path followed by a projectile under the influence of gravity alone, ignoring air resistance.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Trajectory: parabolic path of a projectile.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: students think trajectory is straight. Emphasize that gravity curves all projectile paths.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the motion of a projectile with respect to horizontal and vertical components
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Trajectory is the curved path. Displacement (B) is straight-line distance, not the path.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.structural_anchor: Equations for the horizontal component of motion
questions.question: For a projectile launched horizontally from a height \(h\) with initial speed \(v_{0x}\), which equation correctly gives the horizontal displacement \(\Delta x\) after time \(t\)?
questions.hint: Consider whether there is any horizontal acceleration acting on the projectile.
questions.explanation: The horizontal component of velocity remains constant because gravity acts only vertically, giving \(\Delta x = v_{0x}t\). Option A incorrectly includes gravitational acceleration in the horizontal direction.

source_trace.structural_element: Equations for the horizontal component of motion

explanations_tiered.definition: Horizontal displacement in projectile motion equals horizontal velocity multiplied by time, since horizontal acceleration is zero.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In projectile motion, gravity acts only vertically, so horizontal velocity \(v_{0x}\) remains constant, giving \(\Delta x = v_{0x}t\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine rolling a ball off a table. It moves sideways at constant speed while falling; the sideways distance is just speed times time.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Because there is no horizontal force (ignoring air resistance), horizontal acceleration is zero. Thus \(v_x = v_{0x}\) constant, and \(\Delta x = v_{0x}t\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: From Newton's second law, \(\sum F_x = 0 \Rightarrow a_x = 0\). Integrating twice: \(v_x = v_{0x}\), then \(\Delta x = v_{0x}t\).
reasoning_steps: Identify that no horizontal forces act on the projectile.

formula_analysis.name: Horizontal displacement for constant velocity
formula_analysis.derivation: From \(a_x = 0\), integrate: \(v_x = v_{0x}\), then \(x = x_0 + v_{0x}t\).

misconceptions: Some think gravity affects horizontal motion, but horizontal and vertical components are independent.
Adding \(\frac{1}{2}gt^2\) to horizontal displacement incorrectly mixes vertical acceleration into horizontal motion.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Horizontal: \(\Delta x = v_{0x}t\). No \(g\) in x-direction.

glossary_entry.definition: The distance traveled horizontally by a projectile, equal to horizontal velocity multiplied by time.

flashcard.front: What is the formula for horizontal displacement in projectile motion?
flashcard.back: \(\Delta x = v_{0x}t\), because horizontal velocity is constant (no horizontal acceleration).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is horizontal displacement \(\Delta x = v_{0x}t\) in projectile motion?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No horizontal acceleration (gravity acts vertically), so horizontal velocity is constant: \(\Delta x = v_{0x}t\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The distance traveled horizontally by a projectile, equal to horizontal velocity multiplied by time.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: \(\Delta x = v_{0x}t\). Horizontal motion has constant velocity.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often add \(\frac{1}{2}gt^2\) to horizontal displacement. Emphasize independence of x and y motions.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Derive equations related to projectile motion
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. No horizontal acceleration means constant \(v_x\), so \(\Delta x = v_{0x}t\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For a projectile launched horizontally, the vertical displacement \(\Delta y\) after time \(t\) is given by which equation?
questions.hint: Remember that initial vertical velocity is zero for horizontal launch.
questions.explanation: With \(v_{0y} = 0\) for horizontal projection, the constant-acceleration equation reduces to \(\Delta y = \frac{1}{2}gt^2\). Option D omits the \(\frac{1}{2}\) factor, giving incorrect magnitude.

explanations_tiered.definition: Vertical displacement for horizontally launched projectiles equals one-half times gravitational acceleration times time squared.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Since initial vertical velocity is zero, vertical motion is free fall from rest: \(\Delta y = \frac{1}{2}gt^2\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you drop a ball and also roll one off a table, both fall the same vertical distance in the same time, given by \(\frac{1}{2}gt^2\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: With \(v_{0y}=0\) and constant downward acceleration \(g\), the kinematic equation \(\Delta y = v_{0y}t + \frac{1}{2}gt^2\) simplifies to \(\Delta y = \frac{1}{2}gt^2\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Integrating \(a_y = -g\) twice with \(v_{0y}=0\) and \(y_0=0\): \(v_y = -gt\), then \(y = -\frac{1}{2}gt^2\). Magnitude gives \(\Delta y = \frac{1}{2}gt^2\).
reasoning_steps: Recognize initial vertical velocity \(v_{0y} = 0\) for horizontal launch.
Vertical acceleration is constant: \(a_y = g\) downward.
Apply \(\Delta y = v_{0y}t + \frac{1}{2}a_y t^2\) with \(a_y = g\).

formula_analysis.name: Vertical displacement under constant acceleration from rest
formula_analysis.derivation: From \(a = g\), \(v_0=0\): integrate \(v = gt\), then integrate to get \(y = \frac{1}{2}gt^2\).

misconceptions: Some think horizontal launch affects vertical drop; it does not — vertical motion is independent.
Omitting the \(\frac{1}{2}\) factor is a common algebraic error when using \(\Delta y = gt^2\).

glossary_entry.definition: The vertical distance a horizontally launched projectile falls, given by \(\frac{1}{2}gt^2\).

flashcard.front: What is the vertical displacement for a horizontally launched projectile?
flashcard.back: \(\Delta y = \frac{1}{2}gt^2\) because initial vertical velocity is zero and vertical acceleration is \(g\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is vertical displacement \(\Delta y = \frac{1}{2}gt^2\) for horizontal launch?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Initial vertical velocity is zero, vertical acceleration is \(g\), so \(\Delta y = \frac{1}{2}gt^2\) from kinematics.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The vertical distance a horizontally launched projectile falls, equal to \(\frac{1}{2}gt^2\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that horizontal launch means the projectile is dropped vertically from rest while moving horizontally.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Derive equations related to projectile motion
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. \(v_{0y}=0\) so \(\Delta y = \frac{1}{2}gt^2\). Option D missing \(\frac{1}{2}\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ball is launched horizontally from a height of \(45 \, \text{m}\) above level ground with initial speed \(v_{0x} = 20 \, \text{m/s}\). How long does it take to hit the ground? (Use \(g = 10 \, \text{m/s}^2\))
questions.hint: The time of flight depends only on vertical displacement and gravity, not horizontal speed.
questions.explanation: From \(\Delta y = \frac{1}{2}gt^2\) with \(\Delta y = 45 \, \text{m}\), solving gives \(t = \sqrt{2 \times 45 / 10} = 3.0 \, \text{s}\). Option A incorrectly uses \(\Delta y / v_{0x}\) mixing horizontal and vertical quantities.

explanations_tiered.definition: Time of flight is the duration a projectile remains in the air from launch to landing.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For horizontal projection, time of flight depends only on height and gravity: \(t = \sqrt{2h/g}\), independent of horizontal speed.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine dropping a ball from 45 m — it takes 3 seconds to hit the ground. Rolling it sideways doesn't change the falling time.
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify vertical displacement: \(\Delta y = 45 \, \text{m}\) downward.
reasoning_steps: Recognize that vertical motion determines time of flight.

formula_analysis.name: Time of flight for horizontal launch

misconceptions: Some use horizontal speed to compute time, but time of flight depends only on vertical motion.
Forgetting to take the square root is a common algebraic mistake.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Time of flight for horizontal launch: \(t = \sqrt{2h/g}\). Independent of \(v_{0x}\).

glossary_entry.definition: The total time a projectile remains in the air from launch until it hits the ground.

flashcard.front: How do you calculate time of flight for a horizontally launched projectile?
flashcard.back: \(t = \sqrt{2h/g}\). It depends only on launch height \(h\) and gravity \(g\), not horizontal speed.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A ball is launched horizontally from 45 m height. How does time of flight depend on horizontal speed?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It doesn't. \(t = \sqrt{2h/g} = 3.0 \, \text{s}\) for \(h=45 \, \text{m}\), \(g=10 \, \text{m/s}^2\). Horizontal speed does not affect fall time.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Total time a projectile is in the air; for horizontal launch, \(t = \sqrt{2h/g}\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Classic misconception: students think faster horizontal speed means longer flight time. Emphasize independence.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply equations to solve problems related to projectile motion
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(t = \sqrt{2 \times 45 / 10} = 3.0 \, \text{s}\). Option A is \(h/v_{0x}\), incorrect.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Using the ball from the previous question (launched horizontally from \(45 \, \text{m}\) height with \(v_{0x} = 20 \, \text{m/s}\), \(g = 10 \, \text{m/s}^2\)), what is the horizontal range?
questions.hint: Multiply horizontal speed by the time of flight you calculated in the previous question.
questions.explanation: Range \(R = v_{0x} \times t = 20 \, \text{m/s} \times 3.0 \, \text{s} = 60 \, \text{m}\). Option A incorrectly uses \(t = 2 \, \text{s}\) from a common error in time calculation.

explanations_tiered.definition: Range is the maximum horizontal distance traveled by a projectile from launch point to landing point.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For horizontal projection, range equals horizontal velocity multiplied by time of flight: \(R = v_{0x} \times t\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you roll a ball off a table at 20 m/s and it takes 3 seconds to fall, it will land 60 meters away.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since horizontal velocity is constant, \(R = v_{0x}t\). With \(t = 3.0 \, \text{s}\) from vertical motion, \(R = 20 \times 3.0 = 60 \, \text{m}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Combining \(R = v_{0x}t\) with \(t = \sqrt{2h/g}\) gives \(R = v_{0x}\sqrt{2h/g}\), showing range depends on both height and horizontal speed.
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall time of flight from previous calculation: \(t = 3.0 \, \text{s}\).
reasoning_steps: Find time of flight from vertical motion: \(t = \sqrt{2h/g}\).

formula_analysis.name: Range for horizontal projection
formula_analysis.derivation: From \(\Delta x = v_{0x}t\) with \(\Delta x = R\) when projectile lands.

misconceptions: Some think range depends only on initial speed, but height also matters because it determines time of flight.
Using incorrect time (e.g., 2 s instead of 3 s) is a common arithmetic error.

glossary_entry.definition: The maximum horizontal distance a projectile travels from launch to landing.

flashcard.front: How is range calculated for a horizontally launched projectile?
flashcard.back: \(R = v_{0x}t\), where \(t = \sqrt{2h/g}\). Range depends on height and horizontal speed.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A ball launched horizontally at 20 m/s from 45 m height. What is its range? (g=10 m/s²)
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Maximum horizontal distance traveled by a projectile.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Link to previous question: reinforces that time of flight from Q4 is needed here.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply equations to solve problems related to projectile motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A projectile is launched at an angle \(\theta\) above the horizontal with initial speed \(v_0\). Which statement correctly describes the horizontal component of velocity throughout the flight (ignoring air resistance)?

options.D: It becomes zero at maximum height

questions.hint: Consider what forces act horizontally on the projectile after launch.
questions.explanation: Gravity acts only vertically, so horizontal acceleration is zero, making \(v_x = v_0 \cos \theta\) constant. Option D confuses horizontal velocity with vertical velocity, which is zero at the peak.

source_trace.content_anchor: The horizontal component of the velocity is constant throughout the flight

explanations_tiered.definition: Horizontal velocity component remains constant throughout projectile motion because gravity provides no horizontal force.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Ignoring air resistance, the only force is gravity (vertical), so horizontal acceleration is zero and \(v_x = v_0 \cos \theta\) is constant.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you throw a ball at an angle, its sideways speed doesn't change during flight — only its up/down speed changes because gravity pulls down, not sideways.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Newton's first law: no horizontal force means no horizontal acceleration. Thus \(v_x = v_0 \cos \theta\) = constant at all times.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From \(\sum F_x = 0\), \(a_x = 0\). Integrating twice: \(v_x = \text{constant} = v_0 \cos \theta\); horizontal displacement \(\Delta x = (v_0 \cos \theta)t\).
reasoning_steps: Identify all forces acting on the projectile after launch (gravity only, downward).
Note there is no horizontal force component.
Therefore horizontal velocity remains constant at its initial value \(v_0 \cos \theta\).

formula_analysis.name: Horizontal velocity component
formula_analysis.derivation: Trigonometric resolution of initial velocity vector into components.

misconceptions: Some believe horizontal speed decreases because the projectile slows down overall, but only vertical component changes.
Confusing horizontal velocity with vertical velocity at the peak: vertical velocity becomes zero, not horizontal.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Horizontal velocity constant: \(v_x = v_0 \cos \theta\). No horizontal acceleration.

glossary_entry.definition: The constant horizontal speed of a projectile, equal to \(v_0 \cos \theta\).

flashcard.front: Does horizontal velocity change during projectile motion (no air resistance)? Why?
flashcard.back: No, it remains constant because gravity acts only vertically, so horizontal acceleration is zero.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is horizontal velocity constant in projectile motion?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No horizontal forces (air resistance ignored) → zero horizontal acceleration → \(v_x = v_0 \cos \theta\) constant.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The constant horizontal speed of a projectile, equal to \(v_0 \cos \theta\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: This is a fundamental concept: independence of perpendicular motions.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the motion of a projectile with respect to horizontal and vertical components
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. No horizontal force → \(a_x=0\) → \(v_x\) constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.structural_anchor: Horizontal range and maximum height of a projectile
questions.question: If a projectile is launched with initial speed \(v_0\) at angle \(\theta\) on level ground, the horizontal range \(R\) is given by \(R = \frac{v_0^2 \sin 2\theta}{g}\). For a fixed \(v_0\), at which launch angle does the maximum range occur?
questions.hint: The sine function reaches its maximum value of 1 at \(90^\circ\).
questions.explanation: \(\sin 2\theta\) is maximized when \(2\theta = 90^\circ\) or \(\theta = 45^\circ\). At \(45^\circ\), \(\sin 90^\circ = 1\), giving \(R_{\text{max}} = v_0^2/g\). Options \(30^\circ\) and \(60^\circ\) give the same range (since \(\sin 60^\circ = \sin 120^\circ\)), but less than the maximum.

source_trace.structural_element: Horizontal range and maximum height of a projectile

explanations_tiered.definition: Maximum range for a projectile on level ground occurs at a launch angle of \(45^\circ\) above the horizontal.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Range \(R = (v_0^2 \sin 2\theta)/g\) is maximized when \(\sin 2\theta = 1\), i.e., \(2\theta = 90^\circ\), so \(\theta = 45^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine throwing a ball as far as possible. Throwing too low or too high makes it land closer; the sweet spot is halfway between horizontal and vertical — \(45^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since \(\sin 2\theta\) has a maximum value of 1, setting \(2\theta = 90^\circ\) gives \(\theta = 45^\circ\) for maximum range when launch and landing heights are equal.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Differentiating \(R(\theta) = \frac{v_0^2}{g} \sin 2\theta\) with respect to \(\theta\): \(\frac{dR}{d\theta} = \frac{v_0^2}{g} \cdot 2\cos 2\theta\). Setting derivative to zero gives \(\cos 2\theta = 0 \Rightarrow 2\theta = 90^\circ \Rightarrow \theta = 45^\circ\).
reasoning_steps: For fixed \(v_0\) and \(g\), \(R\) is proportional to \(\sin 2\theta\).

formula_analysis.name: Range for inclined projectile on level ground

misconceptions: Some believe \(30^\circ\) or \(60^\circ\) gives maximum range; they give equal but submaximal range.
Launch angle \(90^\circ\) (straight up) gives zero range, not maximum.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Max range at \(\theta = 45^\circ\) (level ground). Formula: \(R = v_0^2/g\).

glossary_entry.definition: The greatest horizontal distance a projectile can travel, achieved at a \(45^\circ\) launch angle on level ground.

flashcard.front: At what launch angle is projectile range maximized (level ground, same launch/land height)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does a 45° launch angle give maximum range?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Greatest horizontal distance for a projectile, achieved at 45° on level ground.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show that 30° and 60° give same range, both less than at 45°.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply equations to solve problems related to projectile motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the formula for the maximum height \(H\) reached by a projectile launched with speed \(v_0\) at angle \(\theta\) above the horizontal on level ground?
questions.hint: At maximum height, vertical velocity becomes zero. Use \(v_y^2 = v_{0y}^2 + 2a\Delta y\).
questions.explanation: Using \(v_y^2 = v_{0y}^2 - 2gH\) with \(v_y = 0\) and \(v_{0y} = v_0 \sin \theta\) gives \(0 = v_0^2 \sin^2 \theta - 2gH\), so \(H = \frac{v_0^2 \sin^2 \theta}{2g}\). Option A misses the square on sine.

explanations_tiered.definition: Maximum height is the greatest vertical distance reached by a projectile above its launch point.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: At the peak, vertical velocity is zero. Using \(v_y^2 = v_{0y}^2 - 2gH\) with \(v_{0y} = v_0 \sin \theta\) gives \(H = v_0^2 \sin^2 \theta / (2g)\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The higher you throw an object upward, the higher it goes. The initial upward speed is \(v_0 \sin \theta\), and gravity slows it down until it stops at the top.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \(v_y^2 = v_{0y}^2 + 2a_y \Delta y\) with \(a_y = -g\), at maximum height \(v_y = 0\), \(\Delta y = H\), so \(0 = (v_0 \sin \theta)^2 - 2gH \Rightarrow H = \frac{v_0^2 \sin^2 \theta}{2g}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Integrate \(a_y = -g\): \(v_y = v_0 \sin \theta - gt\). Set \(v_y = 0\) → \(t_{\text{peak}} = \frac{v_0 \sin \theta}{g}\). Then integrate again: \(y = (v_0 \sin \theta)t - \frac{1}{2}gt^2\). Substitute \(t_{\text{peak}}\) to get \(H = \frac{v_0^2 \sin^2 \theta}{2g}\).
reasoning_steps: Note vertical velocity at maximum height is zero.

formula_analysis.name: Maximum height of a projectile
formula_analysis.derivation: From conservation of energy: \(\frac{1}{2}mv_{0y}^2 = mgH\) → \(H = v_{0y}^2/(2g) = v_0^2 \sin^2 \theta / (2g)\).

misconceptions: Forgetting to square the sine term is a common algebraic error (using \(\sin \theta\) instead of \(\sin^2 \theta\)).
Some confuse maximum height with range formula.

glossary_entry.definition: The greatest vertical distance above launch point reached by a projectile.

flashcard.front: What is the formula for maximum height of a projectile?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you calculate the maximum height of a projectile?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: \(H = \frac{v_0^2 \sin^2 \theta}{2g}\). Set vertical velocity to zero: \(v_y^2 = v_{0y}^2 - 2gH\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Greatest vertical distance above launch point reached by a projectile.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that at the peak, \(v_y=0\) but \(v_x = v_0 \cos \theta\) remains nonzero.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Derive equations related to projectile motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A projectile is launched from level ground with speed \(v_0 = 30 \, \text{m/s}\) at an angle \(\theta = 30^\circ\) above horizontal. What is its time of flight? (Use \(g = 10 \, \text{m/s}^2\))
questions.explanation: \(t = \frac{2 v_0 \sin \theta}{g} = \frac{2 \times 30 \times \sin 30^\circ}{10} = \frac{60 \times 0.5}{10} = 3.0 \, \text{s}\). Option A incorrectly uses \(v_0 \cos \theta / g\), mixing horizontal and vertical.

explanations_tiered.definition: Time of flight is the total duration a projectile remains in the air from launch to landing at the same vertical level.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For level ground, total flight time = \(2 v_0 \sin \theta / g\), derived from setting vertical displacement to zero in \(\Delta y = v_0 \sin \theta \cdot t - \frac{1}{2}gt^2\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The time the ball spends in the air depends on how high it goes. The initial upward speed is \(v_0 \sin \theta\); gravity slows it up and then speeds it down symmetrically.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(\Delta y = (v_0 \sin \theta)t - \frac{1}{2}gt^2\) with \(\Delta y = 0\) gives \(t(v_0 \sin \theta - \frac{1}{2}gt) = 0\). Nonzero solution: \(t = 2v_0 \sin \theta / g\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Integrate \(a_y = -g\): \(v_y = v_0 \sin \theta - gt\). Set \(\Delta y = \int v_y dt = 0\) over launch to landing. Symmetry gives total time = \(2 \times (v_0 \sin \theta / g) = 2v_0 \sin \theta / g\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Time of flight formula: \(t = 2v_0 \sin \theta / g\).
reasoning_steps: Write vertical displacement equation for launch to landing (same height).
misconceptions: Using \(v_0 \cos \theta\) instead of \(v_0 \sin \theta\) in time of flight formula.
Forgetting the factor of 2 (using \(t = v_0 \sin \theta / g\), which is time to peak only).

glossary_entry.definition: Total time a projectile launched at an angle stays in the air before landing at the same height.

flashcard.front: How do you calculate time of flight for a projectile launched and landing at the same height?
flashcard.back: \(t = 2v_0 \sin \theta / g\). Set \(\Delta y = 0\) in \(\Delta y = v_0 \sin \theta \cdot t - \frac{1}{2}gt^2\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A projectile is launched at 30° with speed 30 m/s. Find time of flight (g=10 m/s²).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Total time a projectile stays in air, \(2v_0 \sin \theta / g\) for level ground.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students that time to peak = \(v_0 \sin \theta / g\); total time is twice that.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply equations to solve problems related to projectile motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.structural_anchor: Relation between range and maximum height
questions.question: For a projectile launched on level ground, the horizontal range \(R\) and maximum height \(H\) are related by \(H = \frac{R \tan \theta}{4}\). A projectile launched at \(30^\circ\) has range \(R\). If the same projectile is launched at \(60^\circ\) with the same speed, how does the new range compare to \(R\)?
questions.explanation: Range \(R \propto \sin 2\theta\). For \(\theta = 30^\circ\), \(\sin 60^\circ = \sqrt{3}/2\); for \(\theta = 60^\circ\), \(\sin 120^\circ = \sqrt{3}/2\) as well. Complementary angles (sum \(90^\circ\)) give the same range. Option A mistakenly thinks \(60^\circ\) doubles the range.

source_trace.structural_element: Relation between range and maximum height

explanations_tiered.definition: Complementary launch angles (\( \theta \) and \(90^\circ - \theta \)) produce the same horizontal range for a given initial speed on level ground.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Since \(R \propto \sin 2\theta\) and \(\sin 2(90^\circ - \theta) = \sin(180^\circ - 2\theta) = \sin 2\theta\), the range is identical for \(30^\circ\) and \(60^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you throw a ball at 30° or 60° with the same force, it lands at the same distance. That’s because both angles give the same horizontal travel time.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For \(\theta = 30^\circ\), \(2\theta = 60^\circ\); for \(\theta = 60^\circ\), \(2\theta = 120^\circ\). \(\sin 60^\circ = \sin 120^\circ = \sqrt{3}/2\), so ranges are equal.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Symmetry: \(R(\theta) = \frac{v_0^2}{g} \sin 2\theta\). Since \(\sin 2(90^\circ - \theta) = \sin(180^\circ - 2\theta) = \sin 2\theta\), we have \(R(\theta) = R(90^\circ - \theta)\) for any \(\theta\).
misconceptions: Some think larger angle always gives shorter range, but \(30^\circ\) and \(60^\circ\) give equal range.
Confusing maximum range (at \(45^\circ\)) with equal-range complementary pairs.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Complementary angles (\(\theta\) and \(90^\circ - \theta\)) give same range.

glossary_entry.definition: Two launch angles that sum to \(90^\circ\) producing the same horizontal range for the same initial speed.

flashcard.front: Do \(30^\circ\) and \(60^\circ\) launch angles give the same range? Why?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why do 30° and 60° launch angles give the same range?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Range ∝ sin 2θ. sin 60° = sin 120°, and 2×60°=120°, so ranges are equal.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Two angles summing to 90° that yield identical projectile range.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use this to challenge the intuition that higher angle always means shorter distance.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the motion of a projectile with respect to horizontal and vertical components
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. sin 60° = sin 120° → R(30°) = R(60°).
